evidence.reasoning: The first-person 'we' includes the author among genuine believers describing a real possibility of forfeiting the only available atonement.

evidence.quote: For {it is} impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost,
evidence.reasoning: The cumulative regenerate-sounding descriptors most naturally denote genuine believers, not mere professors.

evidence.quote: Wherefore the rather, brethren, give diligence to make your calling and election sure: for if ye do these things, ye shall never fall:
evidence.reasoning: The conditional exhortation implies perseverance is not automatic but requires ongoing diligence.

source_position_labels: Arminian/Wesleyan: genuinely regenerate believers can apostatize and lose salvation

positions.label: Loss of reward/fellowship, not salvation itself
positions.summary: The warning targets severe temporal discipline and loss of fellowship/reward for willful ongoing sin, while the sanctified standing already secured 'once for all' (10:10, 10:14) is not itself forfeited; the priestly contrast (10:11-12) underscores that Christ's single offering has already accomplished what repeated sacrifices could not.
positions.why_not_higher: The plain sense of 'no more sacrifice for sins' and Hebrews' surrounding fearful-judgment language reads more naturally as ultimate judgment; the retrieved evidence lacks 10:35's explicit reward vocabulary to anchor this view, and the scope of 'loss' in 10:11-12 remains ambiguous between the two voices.
positions.confidence_rationale: This view resolves the tension between 10:14's security and 10:26's warning, but the 'reward' language that typically supports it elsewhere in Hebrews is absent from this evidence set; both voices rate it medium confidence at best.
positions.weakest_link: Without Heb 10:35 ('cast not away...great recompence of reward') in evidence, the reading rests more on systematic harmonization than on directly cited reward-language in this passage; interpreting the cessation of sacrifice as primarily disciplinary rather than salvific is an assumption, not a stated conclusion.
positions.what_would_change_this: Inclusion of Hebrews 10:32-39 (reward/confidence language), 1 Corinthians 3:12-15 (saved 'as through fire'), or explicit biblical language linking temporal loss with eternal security would substantially strengthen this position's direct textual base.
interpretive_moves: Treats 10:10/10:14's 'once for all' sanctification as settling ultimate salvation status prior to the warning
Reads 'no more sacrifice for sins' in 10:26 as loss of temporal/relational benefit rather than forensic justification
Views 10:11-12's priestly contrast as a typological shift from ineffective to effective means, not a statement about individual security
Assumes systematic harmonization with reward-passages elsewhere in Hebrews not present in the retrieved set

evidence.reasoning: The 'once for all' sanctifying offering is already accomplished, so 10:26's warning must target something less than that settled sanctification.

evidence.quote: And every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins:
evidence.reasoning: Emphasizes the futility of repeated sacrifices, contrasting with the superior once-for-all offering already secured for believers.

evidence.quote: But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God;

source_position_labels: Loss of reward/fellowship, not salvation itself

positions.label: Hypothetical/pastoral warning: real-but-notional danger for rhetorical effect
positions.summary: The conditional warnings (10:26, 6:4) function pastorally to spur watchfulness and are framed as a real category of danger without asserting that any actual believer among the readers has or will fall into it; the conditional grammar ('if we sin wilfully') suggests a cautionary illustration rather than a predicted or guaranteed outcome.
positions.why_not_higher: Hebrews' grammatical mood does not by itself establish irreality; many grammarians treat these as open, real conditions addressed to a genuinely at-risk audience, making a purely hypothetical reading a minority view; both voices note it rests on thin, indirect support.
positions.confidence_rationale: The view coherently reconciles warning-texts with security-promises but rests heavily on a rhetorical judgment about conditional clauses not demonstrable from these isolated verses alone; both voices rate it low-to-medium confidence.
positions.weakest_link: Hebrews' repeated, urgent, escalating warnings (6:4, 10:26) read more naturally as addressing a real danger the audience faced than as purely rhetorical hypotheticals; the surrounding context lacks explicit pastoral commentary framing the warning as hypothetical.
positions.what_would_change_this: Access to the fuller warning unit, such as Heb 6:9's expressed confidence that better things accompany the readers, or explicit pastoral commentary framing 10:26 as illustrative rather than descriptive, would strengthen the hypothetical reading.
interpretive_moves: Treats Hebrews' conditional 'if' clauses as rhetorical devices for pastoral effect rather than descriptions of a live threat
Relies on the author's positive confidence elsewhere in Hebrews (not present in this evidence) to argue the warnings are not expected to materialize
Harmonizes with unconditional security promises by making the warned-of scenario purely notional

nodes.label: Conditional grammar alone doesn't establish irreality
nodes.detail: Greek subjunctive conditions can describe real possibilities.

positions.label: Corporate/covenantal apostasy view
positions.summary: Warnings address the corporate faithfulness of the new-covenant community rather than individual eternal destiny; apostasy affects the community's standing without necessarily determining each individual's personal salvation.
positions.why_not_higher: Named by only one voice, and the internalization language of 10:15-16 (law written on individual hearts and minds) suggests a personal covenant relationship, limiting a purely corporate reading; the other voice considered this option but declined to give it standalone status, citing insufficient OT covenant-corpus evidence in the retrieved set.
positions.confidence_rationale: Low — the passage leans toward individualized promises of law in the heart, complicating a fully corporate interpretation, and only one voice supplied direct textual support.
positions.weakest_link: Assuming Heb 10:15-16 is primarily about corporate rather than individual covenant fulfillment; the retrieved evidence lacks the broader OT covenant-corpus texts needed to establish this reading robustly.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit corporate language affirming that apostasy affects the community without affecting personal salvation, or inclusion of the broader OT covenant background, would raise this position's weight and evidentiary standing.
interpretive_moves: Interprets Heb 10:15-16 as a corporate promise of internalized law for the covenant community
Reads warnings (6:4; 10:26) as addressing communal backsliding rather than individual final destiny

nodes.label: Universal perfection claim for sanctified believers (10:14)
nodes.detail: Individual-perfection language cuts against a purely corporate reading.

evidence.quote: {Whereof} the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us: for after that he had said before,
evidence.reasoning: Introduces the witness of the Holy Spirit, hinting at a corporate covenant acknowledgment.

evidence.quote: This {is} the covenant that I will make with them after those days, saith the Lord, I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them;
evidence.reasoning: Corporate promise of internalized law for the new-covenant community.

synthesis.dissent_notes: This is a two-voice synthesis with a narrow structural disagreement over how many distinct positions exist: the Claude voice folded 'apostates were merely professing / never regenerate' into its single Reformed position, while the local (granite4.1:8b) voice split this into two separate named positions ('Warning as means of perseverance' and 'Apostasy of merely professing'). This synthesis merged them into one Reformed cluster since both describe the same conclusion (6:4/10:26 apostates lacked saving union) using the same core verses — this materially inflates the Reformed cluster's weight (0.44) relative to what either voice individually assigned (0.34 and 0.36/0.18 respectively) and should be read as an interpretive judgment, not a neutral fact. The 'Corporate/covenantal apostasy view' was named only by the local voice; the Claude voice explicitly considered and declined to elevate it to standalone status, citing thin evidence (a single supporting verse, no broader OT covenant corpus) rather than rejecting its substance — it is preserved here at half the originating voice's weight per the dissent-preservation rule, but should be treated as the weakest-evidenced position in the set. The margin between the top position (Reformed, 0.44) and the second (Arminian, 0.24) is real but not razor-thin; however, no position achieves an outright majority, and both voices independently cap their own confidence at 'medium,' so this synthesis does likewise. The local (granite4.1:8b) voice's weighting also shows internal normalization artifacts (raw weights summing to 1.1 before renormalization), suggesting somewhat less precise weight calibration than the Claude voice — a caution against over-reading small weight differences.
unresolved_tensions: Hebrews 10:14's 'perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (completed, secure status) sits in tension with 10:26's warning that willful sin after 'receiving the knowledge of the truth' leaves 'no more sacrifice for sins.'

Hebrews 6:4's fourfold description (enlightened, tasted the heavenly gift, partakers of the Holy Ghost) is read by some as proof of genuine regeneration and by others as proof only of external covenant exposure; neither voice's evidence set can settle which reading is correct.

John 10:28 and Philippians 1:6 promise unconditional completion/preservation, while 2 Peter 1:10's 'give diligence to make your calling and election sure' implies some contingency, and the evidence does not clarify how these cohere.

Whether Hebrews 10:15-16's covenant/internalized-law language is corporate (addressed to the community) or individual (addressed to each believer's heart) remains unresolved and was supported by only one voice.
synthesis.synthesis: Both voices agree that Hebrews 10 (especially 10:14 and 10:26, read together with 6:4) is the crux text in the perseverance debate, and both independently rank a Reformed/effectual-warning reading as the plurality position, an Arminian/genuine-apostasy reading as the strongest rival, and 'loss of reward, not salvation' and 'hypothetical/pastoral warning' as plausible but evidentially thinner mediating options. The central interpretive fork is whether Hebrews 6:4's experiential vocabulary ('enlightened,' 'tasted,' 'partakers of the Holy Ghost') denotes genuine regeneration (favoring the Arminian reading) or merely external covenant participation (favoring the Reformed reading) — this single exegetical decision drives nearly all downstream disagreement, including how 10:26's 'no more sacrifice for sins' is scoped. The voices diverge mainly in granularity: the local voice split the Reformed position into two named sub-arguments and additionally surfaced a corporate/covenantal reading that the other voice considered but declined to elevate for lack of supporting OT-covenant evidence. Neither voice's retrieved evidence includes key co-text often invoked in this debate (Heb 6:5-6, 10:29, 10:32-39, or the broader OT covenant corpus), which caps confidence at medium for all positions and leaves the reward/fellowship and hypothetical-warning views under-evidenced relative to their theological plausibility.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: Both contributing voices independently rated their overall confidence as medium, and per the lowest-common-denominator rule this synthesis does the same. The evidence directly engages the core crux (10:14 vs. 6:4/10:26) with clear cross-position tension and several strong proof-texts on each side, but both voices flag that key co-text (Heb 6:5-6, 10:29, 10:32-39 reward language, OT covenant background) was not retrieved, and the local (granite4.1:8b) voice shows minor internal weight-normalization looseness (raw weights summing to 1.1) and a coarser evidence-citation style (several positions cite only one verse directly), which further caps confidence rather than raising it.

research_trail.label: Question framed
research_trail.detail: Scoped Hebrews 10 within the perseverance-of-the-saints debate; both voices independently identified 4-6 candidate positions to evaluate.

research_trail.label: Evidence retrieved
research_trail.detail: Both voices pulled Hebrews 10:1-16 plus 10:26, cross-referenced with John 10:27-28, Phil 1:6, 1 John 5:11, Heb 6:4, 2 Peter 1:10, Rom 8:31.

research_trail.label: Classified sacrificial background as low-yield
research_trail.detail: Heb 10:1-9 provide christological/sacrificial background but were not directly load-bearing for any final position in either voice.

research_trail.label: Identified the exegetical crux
research_trail.detail: Both voices independently identified Heb 10:14's 'perfected for ever' versus 10:26/6:4's warning language as the central fork driving all positions.

research_trail.label: Reformed voice constructed (both voices)
research_trail.detail: Both voices built an effectual-warning/never-truly-regenerate reading using 10:14, John 10:28, and a reinterpretation of 6:4/10:26; the local voice split this into two named sub-positions, later merged in synthesis.
research_trail.related_position: Reformed/Calvinist: warnings are effectual means; apostates were never truly regenerate

research_trail.label: Arminian voice constructed (both voices)
research_trail.detail: Both voices built the genuine-loss position from 10:26's 'we' and 6:4's regenerate-sounding descriptors.
research_trail.related_position: Arminian/Wesleyan: genuinely regenerate believers can apostatize and lose salvation

research_trail.label: Mediating positions constructed
research_trail.detail: Both voices built reward/fellowship and hypothetical-warning readings as harmonizing strategies; the local voice additionally surfaced a corporate/covenantal reading not elevated by the other voice.

research_trail.label: Cross-voice clustering and weight averaging
research_trail.detail: Synthesis merged the local voice's split Reformed sub-positions into one cluster, averaged weights across both voices per position, and preserved the corporate/covenantal view at half-weight as single-voice dissent.

research_trail.label: Limitations noted

nodes.label: 'Perfected for ever' establishes permanent standing
nodes.detail: Reinforces that the sanctified state itself is stable.

nodes.label: Heb 6:4's severity suggests more than lost fellowship
nodes.detail: The 'impossible to renew' language implies ultimate stakes.

nodes.label: Key reward vocabulary (10:35) absent from evidence set
nodes.detail: Position leans on text not retrieved here.

evidence.quote: there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins
evidence.reasoning: Read as loss of the ongoing benefit of cleansing/fellowship for continued willful sin, not forfeiture of the once-for-all sanctified standing.

evidence.quote: By the which will we are sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.
evidence.reasoning: The 'once for all' sanctifying offering is already accomplished, so 10:26's warning must target something less than that settled sanctification.

evidence.quote: For by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified.
evidence.reasoning: Reinforces that the sanctified state is permanent, pushing 10:26's referent toward loss of fellowship or reward rather than salvation.

positions.label: Hypothetical/pastoral warning: real-but-notional danger for rhetorical effect
positions.summary: The conditional warnings (10:26, 6:4) function pastorally to spur watchfulness and are framed as a real category of danger without asserting that any actual believer among the readers has or will fall into it.
positions.why_not_higher: Hebrews' grammatical mood does not by itself establish irreality; many grammarians treat these as open, real conditions addressed to a genuinely at-risk audience, making a purely hypothetical reading a minority view.
positions.confidence_rationale: The view coherently reconciles warning-texts with security-promises but rests heavily on a rhetorical judgment about conditional clauses not demonstrable from these isolated verses alone.
positions.weakest_link: Hebrews' repeated, urgent, escalating warnings (6:4, 10:26) read more naturally as addressing a real danger the audience faced than as purely rhetorical hypotheticals.
positions.what_would_change_this: Access to the fuller warning unit, such as Heb 6:9's expressed confidence that better things accompany the readers, would strengthen the hypothetical reading; it is absent from this evidence set.
interpretive_moves: Treats Hebrews' conditional 'if' clauses as rhetorical devices for pastoral effect rather than descriptions of a live threat
Relies on the author's positive confidence elsewhere in Hebrews (not present in this evidence) to argue the warnings are not expected to materialize
Harmonizes with unconditional security promises by making the warned-of scenario purely notional

nodes.label: Warnings describe a real-but-hypothetical danger
nodes.detail: Serves pastoral, not descriptive, purpose.

nodes.label: Security promises assume perseverance as the norm
nodes.detail: God's completion of the good work supports non-realization of the warning.

nodes.label: Severity/detail of 6:4/10:26 suggest a live danger
nodes.detail: The specificity argues against pure hypothesis.

nodes.label: Conditional grammar alone doesn't establish irreality
nodes.detail: Greek subjunctive conditions can describe real possibilities.

evidence.quote: For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins,
evidence.reasoning: The conditional 'if' framing functions rhetorically to spur watchfulness rather than reporting actual apostasy among the readers.

evidence.quote: For it is impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost,
evidence.reasoning: Framed as an extreme case used pedagogically to warn, not to assert any actual instance among the addressees.

evidence.quote: Being confident of this very thing, that he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ:
evidence.reasoning: Grounds the expectation that genuine believers will in fact persevere, making the warned-of scenario hypothetical rather than realized.

result.dissent_notes: The candidate 'merely professing (non-regenerate)' argument is folded into the Reformed position as a sub-argument rather than a standalone position, since it deploys the same verses (6:4, 10:26) toward the same conclusion under a different anthropology, with no distinct verse citation in this evidence set. A corporate/covenantal apostasy view (warnings addressing the covenant community's national/corporate faithfulness, drawing on Heb 10:16's new-covenant language) was considered but not given standalone position status because the retrieved evidence lacks the broader OT covenant-corpus texts needed to establish it robustly beyond a single supporting verse.
unresolved_tensions: Hebrews 10:14's 'perfected for ever them that are sanctified' (completed, secure status) sits in tension with 10:26's warning that willful sin after 'receiving the knowledge of the truth' leaves 'no more sacrifice for sins.'

Hebrews 6:4's fourfold description (enlightened, tasted the heavenly gift, partakers of the Holy Ghost) is read by some as proof of genuine regeneration and by others as proof only of external covenant exposure, and the evidence set alone cannot settle which reading is correct.

John 10:28 and Philippians 1:6 promise unconditional completion/preservation, while 2 Peter 1:10's 'give diligence to make your calling and election sure' implies some contingency, and the evidence does not clarify how these cohere.
result.synthesis: The strongest Reformed reading leans on 10:14's 'perfected for ever' plus John 10:28's unloseable-sheep promise, reinterpreting 6:4/10:26 as describing covenant-exposed non-elect; the strongest Arminian reading leans on the natural, cumulative regenerate-sounding language of 6:4 and the first-person 'we' of 10:26, treating security promises as implicitly conditional. The central interpretive tension is whether 6:4's experiential vocabulary (enlightened, tasted, partakers) denotes genuine regeneration or merely external covenant participation — that single exegetical fork drives nearly the entire downstream disagreement, with the reward/fellowship and hypothetical-warning views offering mediating strategies that preserve both the security texts and the warning's seriousness without fully resolving the fork.
result.confidence_rationale: The evidence directly engages the core crux (10:14 vs. 6:4/10:26) with clear cross-position tension and several strong proof-texts on each side, but the retrieved set omits key co-text (Heb 6:6, 10:29, 10:32-39) that traditions typically invoke, and the OT covenant background for corporate readings is absent, preventing a high-confidence verdict.

positions.label: Loss of salvation (Arminian/Wesleyan)
positions.summary: Hebrews 10 indicates that regenerate believers can wilfully sin and be lost, implying perseverance is not guaranteed.
positions.why_not_higher: The verse about no more sacrifice after wilful sin (Heb 10:26) suggests a possible loss of salvation, but the surrounding context emphasizes final judgment and does not explicitly state that true believers are excluded.
positions.confidence_rationale: Medium – The warning in Heb 10:26 is clear, yet its scope (wilful sin vs. genuine apostasy) remains debated among interpreters.
positions.weakest_link: Interpretation of 'no more sacrifice' as a loss of salvation rather than a cessation of means.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit biblical affirmation that regenerate believers cannot be lost would increase the weight.
interpretive_moves: Read Heb 10:26 as a conditional warning about wilful sin leading to no further atonement.
Treat 'no more sacrifice' as a cessation of means, not a guarantee of loss.

nodes.label: Wilful sin after knowledge removes further sacrifice.

evidence.quote: For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins.
evidence.reasoning: Suggests a loss of means to atone, implying possible loss of salvation for those who persist in sin.

positions.label: Warning as means of perseverance (Reformed/Calvinist)
positions.summary: The warnings in Hebrews are real and effective, ensuring the elect persevere to the end.
positions.why_not_higher: While Heb 10:14 affirms perfected believers, the conditional warning in Heb 10:26 introduces a potential exception.
positions.confidence_rationale: Medium – Strong support from Hebrews' affirmation of perseverance (Heb 10:14) tempered by the conditional nature of Heb 10:26.
positions.weakest_link: Balancing the universal perfection claim against a conditional warning about wilful sin.
positions.what_would_change_this: A clearer biblical statement that no regenerate believer can fall away would raise the weight.
interpretive_moves: Interpret Heb 10:14 as a universal truth for all who are sanctified.
Read Heb 10:26 as addressing those outside the covenant or those who have apostatized, not true believers.

nodes.label: The elect persevere through God's warnings.
Perfection of believers by Christ's offering (Heb 10:14).

evidence.quote: For by one offering he hath perfected for ever them that are sanctified.
evidence.reasoning: Affirms universal perfection of believers through Christ's sacrifice.

evidence.quote: {Whereof} the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us: for after that he had said before, This {is} the covenant that I will make with them after those days, saith the Lord, I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them;
evidence.reasoning: Indicates internalization of God's law, supporting ongoing perseverance.

positions.label: Apostasy of merely professing (non-regenerate)
positions.summary: Those described as falling away were never truly regenerate but were outwardly identified with the covenant community.
positions.why_not_higher: The passage about enlightenment and tasting the heavenly gift (Heb 6:4) suggests a prior experience, but does not definitively categorize them as non‑regenerate.
positions.confidence_rationale: Low – The evidence is indirect; it hints at a loss of regeneration but lacks explicit affirmation.
positions.weakest_link: Assuming Heb 6:4 refers exclusively to non‑regenerate believers without clear biblical precedent.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit scriptural identification of those who fall away as never truly regenerate would strengthen the position.
interpretive_moves: Treat Heb 6:4 as describing a once‑enlightened state that can be lost.
Interpret Heb 10:26 as applying to this group rather than true believers.

nodes.label: Fallen away are merely professing, not regenerate.
Enlightened but tasted the gift and partook of the Holy Ghost can fall away (Heb 6:4).

evidence.quote: For {it is} impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost,
evidence.reasoning: Implies a prior state that can be lost, suggesting non‑regenerate or backslidden believers.

evidence.quote: For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins.
evidence.reasoning: Conditional warning aligns with a group that has already been enlightened but may fall away.

positions.label: Loss of reward/fellowship, not salvation
positions.summary: Hebrews 10 warns of consequences (loss of fellowship, discipline) rather than eternal loss.
positions.why_not_higher: While Heb 10:11-12 contrast the old and new covenants, they do not explicitly address eternal security versus temporal discipline.
positions.confidence_rationale: Medium – The contrast between repeated sacrifices and a single offering hints at differing outcomes, but the scope of 'loss' remains ambiguous.
positions.weakest_link: Interpreting the cessation of sacrifice as primarily disciplinary rather than salvific.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit biblical language linking temporal loss with eternal security would bolster this view.
interpretive_moves: View Heb 10:11-12 as a typological shift from ineffective to effective means.
Read Heb 10:26 as referring to loss of covenant privileges, not eternal salvation.

nodes.label: Warnings concern discipline and reward, not eternal fate.
Conditional warning about no more sacrifice (Heb 10:26).

evidence.quote: And every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins:
evidence.reasoning: Emphasizes futility of repeated sacrifices, hinting at a superior once‑for‑all sacrifice.

evidence.quote: But this man, after he had offered one sacrifice for sins for ever, sat down on the right hand of God;
evidence.reasoning: Contrasts perpetual priesthood with a single, eternal offering.

positions.label: Corporate/covenantal apostasy view
positions.summary: Warnings address the corporate faithfulness of Israel rather than individual eternal destiny.
positions.why_not_higher: The internalization language (Heb 10:15‑16) suggests personal covenant relationship, limiting a purely corporate reading.
positions.confidence_rationale: Low – The passage leans toward individualized promises of law in the heart, complicating a fully corporate interpretation.
positions.weakest_link: Assuming Heb 10:15‑16 is primarily about corporate rather than individual covenant fulfillment.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit corporate language affirming that apostasy affects the community without affecting personal salvation would raise the weight.
interpretive_moves: Interpret Heb 10:15‑16 as a corporate promise of internalized law for Israel.
Read warnings (Heb 6:4; 10:26) as addressing communal backsliding.

nodes.label: Universal perfection claim for sanctified believers (Heb 10:14).

evidence.quote: {Whereof} the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us:
evidence.reasoning: Introduces the witness of the Holy Spirit, hinting at corporate acknowledgment.

evidence.quote: This {is} the covenant that I will make with them after those days, saith the Lord, I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them;
evidence.reasoning: Corporate promise of internalized law for Israel.

positions.label: Hypothetical warning view
positions.summary: Warnings serve a pastoral/pedagogical function, describing a real but hypothetical danger without asserting actual apostasy among believers.
positions.why_not_higher: The conditional nature of Heb 10:26 limits its applicability to true believers; thus the view remains a minor nuance.
positions.confidence_rationale: Low – The verse is brief and does not expand on hypothetical usage, leaving much interpretation open.
positions.weakest_link: Lack of explicit pastoral language in the surrounding context.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit pastoral commentary framing Heb 10:26 as a hypothetical warning would increase weight.
interpretive_moves: Read Heb 10:26 as a rhetorical illustration rather than a doctrinal guarantee.
Treat it as a cautionary example for readers.

nodes.label: Warnings are hypothetical, not predictive of actual apostasy.

evidence.quote: For if we sin wilfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins.
evidence.reasoning: Conditional language suggests a hypothetical scenario rather than an inevitable outcome.

result.dissent_notes: The retrieved verses provide mixed signals: Heb 10:14 affirms universal perfection, while Heb 10:26 offers a conditional warning that can be read either as addressing non‑regenerate believers or as a hypothetical pastoral caution. The lack of explicit corporate language limits the corporate view, and the Arminian position hinges on interpreting a single conditional verse without broader scriptural corroboration.
unresolved_tensions: Scope of Hebrews 10:26 – Is it limited to non‑regenerate or merely hypothetical?
Interpretation of 'perfected for ever' (Heb 10:14) as universal versus selective.
Whether the warnings primarily address corporate covenant faithfulness or individual perseverance.
result.synthesis: The strongest Reformed view emphasizes Hebrews 10:14's affirmation of universal perfection, tempered by the conditional warning in Heb 10:26 which may apply to those who have never truly entered the covenant. The Arminian perspective holds that genuine believers can apostatize, relying on Heb 6:4 and Heb 10:26 as evidence of possible loss. Corporate and hypothetical readings offer nuanced pastoral applications but lack decisive scriptural affirmation.
result.confidence_rationale: The evidence includes key verses supporting both perseverance (Heb 10:14) and conditional warnings (Heb 10:26), yet the scope of these warnings remains debated, leading to moderate confidence in delineating positions.

overreach: The Reformed position conflates 'enlightened'/'partakers of the Holy Ghost' with mere external covenant participation without sufficient OT-covenant evidence.
The Arminian reading treats Hebrews 6:4 as describing genuine regeneration despite the lack of explicit NT corroboration beyond this passage.
judge.notes: All cited verses are present in the provided evidence, so grounding is faithful. However, the Reformed and Arminian positions overreach by interpreting ambiguous covenant language (Heb 6:4) to assert distinct theological conclusions about regeneration versus non-regeneration, respectively. The balance is compromised because the strongest rival (Arminian) is not equally substantiated by the same textual nuances that support the plurality position (Reformed), leading to a perceived flattening of genuine minority views.

kill_test.target_label: Reformed/Calvinist: warnings are effectual means; apostates were never truly regenerate
kill_test.strongest_counter: Hebrews 6:4 presents a stark warning that those who have been "enlightened," tasted the "heavenly gift," and partaken of the Holy Spirit can still fall away, suggesting they were genuinely regenerated at some point. This directly contradicts the Reformed/Calvinist claim that apostates were never truly regenerate; it implies genuine regeneration followed by defection, which undermines the position's core assertion that warnings are effectual means preserving only those who were never truly in the covenant. The verse thus forces a reinterpretation or renders the 'merely professing' reading untenable.
kill_test.vulnerable_claim: Warnings (e.g., Hebrews 6:4, 10:26) function as God-appointed, effectual means of preserving true believers, exposing that those who fall away were only outwardly identified with the covenant community and never possessed saving, regenerating union.
kill_test.notes: The verse Hebrews 6:4 provides a clear biblical precedent for genuine regeneration followed by apostasy, directly challenging the Reformed/Calvinist position that such individuals were never truly regenerate. This makes the core claim fatally undermined.

results.question: What does the New Testament teach about divorce and remarriage?

evidence.text: They said, “Moses allowed a certificate of divorce to be written, and to divorce her.”
They asked him, “Why then did Moses command us to give her a certificate of divorce and divorce her?”
“ Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery. He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
By so much was Jesus made a surety of a better testament.
Whereupon neither the first {testament} was dedicated without blood. {dedicated: or, purified}
Saying, This {is} the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you.
I tell you that whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, and marries another, commits adultery; and he who marries her when she is divorced commits adultery.”

And for this cause he is the mediator of the new testament, that by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions {that were} under the first testament, they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance.
For where a testament is, there must of necessity be the death of him that made it.
And he said unto them, This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many.
If a woman herself divorces her husband and marries another, she commits adultery.”
For this is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many for the remission of sins.
He said to them, “ Whoever divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery against her.
Likewise also the cup after supper, saying, This cup {is} the new testament in my blood, which is shed for you.
For a testament {is} of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth.
It hath been said, Whosoever shall put away his wife, let him give her a writing of divorcement:
Pharisees came to him testing him, and asked him, “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife?”

positions.label: Exception for sexual immorality permits both divorce and remarriage
positions.summary: Jesus's teaching as recorded in Matthew explicitly carves out sexual immorality (porneia) as a legitimate ground for divorce, and both voices read the exception clause as extending to remarriage rights as well. This is the most directly textually-supported position and the one both voices weighted most heavily.
positions.why_not_higher: Mark and Luke record the same core teaching with no exception clause at all, so this view must explain why Matthew alone preserves a qualifier the other Synoptics omit; it also does not, on its own, address broader grounds such as abandonment or abuse.
positions.confidence_rationale: Direct verbal support exists in Matt 19:9 and is affirmed independently by both voices, but the parallel Gospel silence and grammatical ambiguity about clause-scope keep it short of decisive.
positions.weakest_link: Whether 'except for sexual immorality' grammatically licenses the following 'and marries another' clause, or only excuses the divorce itself, is disputed among careful readers of the Greek.
positions.what_would_change_this: A clearer parallel text (e.g., explicit remarriage language tied to the exception, or 1 Corinthians 7 material) would settle the scope question.
interpretive_moves: Matthew is read as clarifying/completing Mark's more absolute-sounding saying rather than conflicting with it
Porneia is read broadly as marital sexual unfaithfulness rather than narrowly as incest
The exception clause is taken to modify the whole sentence, including remarriage, not just the act of divorcing

nodes.label: Exception permits remarriage
nodes.detail: Sexual immorality is a valid ground for both divorce and remarriage.

nodes.label: Explicit exception clause
nodes.detail: Matt 19:9 states the exception directly in the same sentence as remarriage language.

nodes.label: Mark/Luke omit exception
nodes.detail: Parallel sayings give an unqualified prohibition.

nodes.label: Grammatical scope disputed
nodes.detail: Does the exception modify only 'divorces' or the whole clause including remarriage?

evidence.quote: I tell you that whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, and marries another, commits adultery; and he who marries her when she is divorced commits adultery.”
evidence.reasoning: Jesus explicitly names sexual immorality as an exception within the same sentence addressing remarriage; both voices treat this as the anchor text.

evidence.quote: They asked him, “Why then did Moses command us to give her a certificate of divorce and divorce her?”
evidence.reasoning: Sets up the Mosaic background against which Jesus's exception is offered as a corrective standard.

evidence.quote: It hath been said, Whosoever shall put away his wife, let him give her a writing of divorcement:
evidence.reasoning: Establishes the legal Mosaic practice Jesus is reforming.

source_position_labels: Exception for sexual immorality permits both divorce and remarriage (Claude Code sonnet)
Exception for sexual immorality (permits remarriage) (Local granite4.1:8b)

positions.label: Marriage is indissoluble: no remarriage while a spouse lives
positions.summary: Mark and Luke report Jesus's teaching with no exception at all — divorcing and remarrying is adultery, meaning the first marriage bond remains binding for life. Both voices independently identified this position from the unqualified Synoptic parallels.
positions.why_not_higher: Matthew's Gospel, equally scriptural, explicitly records an exception clause this view must treat as non-normative or narrower; one voice notes only two verses directly and explicitly state the strict prohibition, so this reading leans on cross-referencing Mark 10:2-4's framing to strengthen its case.
positions.confidence_rationale: Strong, repeated, unqualified language in two independent Gospel traditions (Mark, Luke) supports this reading directly, though it must explain away Matthew's differing wording.
positions.weakest_link: Must account for why Matthew's version differs from Mark and Luke without simply dismissing Matthew's wording.
positions.what_would_change_this: If Matt 19:9's exception clause were shown to concern a narrower category (e.g., incestuous unions) rather than general infidelity, this view would gain further support.
interpretive_moves: Mark and Luke are treated as preserving the fuller, unqualified force of Jesus's teaching
Matthew's exception is read narrowly (e.g., referring to unlawful/incestuous unions rather than general adultery)
'Commits adultery' is read as describing an ongoing state, implying the original marriage bond persists

nodes.label: No remarriage permitted
nodes.detail: The marriage bond is indissoluble; remarriage after divorce is adultery.

nodes.label: Unqualified prohibition in Mark
nodes.detail: Mark 10:11-12 states divorce-and-remarry is adultery for both husband and wife, with no exception.

nodes.label: Luke's parallel confirms
nodes.detail: Luke 16:18 repeats the same unqualified rule.

nodes.label: Matthew's exception clause
nodes.detail: Matt 19:9 records an exception Mark/Luke omit.

evidence.quote: He said to them, “ Whoever divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery against her.
evidence.reasoning: States the prohibition without any stated exception, implying universal scope.

evidence.quote: If a woman herself divorces her husband and marries another, she commits adultery.”
evidence.reasoning: Extends the same absolute rule symmetrically to wives.

evidence.quote: “ Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery. He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
evidence.reasoning: An independent Gospel tradition confirms the same unqualified teaching.

source_position_labels: Marriage is indissoluble: no remarriage while a spouse lives (Claude Code sonnet)

positions.label: Exception justifies divorce only, not remarriage
positions.summary: Even granting Matthew's sexual-immorality exception as valid grounds for divorce, the adultery language in Mark and Luke is read as still barring remarriage, so the innocent party should remain single or reconcile. Named only by one voice; preserved as dissent per synthesis rules.
positions.why_not_higher: The more natural surface reading of Matt 19:9's clause structure, held by many interpreters, ties the exception to the whole consequence (both divorce and freedom to remarry), not divorce alone; this position was also not independently corroborated by the second voice.
positions.confidence_rationale: A coherent harmonizing move but rests on a technical, minority grammatical argument about clause scope, and single-voice attestation further caps confidence.
positions.weakest_link: Requires reading Matthew's exception as narrowly restricted to the legality of the divorce action while still applying Mark/Luke's blanket adultery language to any subsequent remarriage.
positions.what_would_change_this: Clearer evidence about Greek clause-scope conventions, or a text explicitly separating 'freedom to divorce' from 'freedom to remarry,' would help decide this question.
interpretive_moves: Matt 19:9's exception is read as modifying only the legitimacy of the divorce, not a license to remarry
Mark 10:11 and Luke 16:18's blanket adultery language is treated as still governing remarriage even after a justified divorce

nodes.label: Divorce may be justified, remarriage still barred
nodes.detail: Sexual immorality justifies separation but not a new marriage.

nodes.label: Universal adultery language
nodes.detail: Mark 10:12's parallel wife/husband language suggests no remarriage exception was intended.

nodes.label: Exception clause reads naturally as covering remarriage
nodes.detail: Most straightforward reading of Matt 19:9 ties exception to the whole verse.

evidence.quote: If a woman herself divorces her husband and marries another, she commits adultery.”
evidence.reasoning: Shows the adultery-upon-remarriage principle applies symmetrically and without qualification even where divorce itself may be understandable.

evidence.quote: I tell you that whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, and marries another, commits adultery; and he who marries her when she is divorced commits adultery.”
evidence.reasoning: Read narrowly, the exception is taken to excuse only the act of divorcing.

source_position_labels: Exception justifies divorce only, not remarriage (Claude Code sonnet only — not named by Local voice)

positions.label: Normative ideal with pastoral/situational discretion in application
positions.summary: Both voices converge on a position that treats lifelong marriage as the norm while leaving application to pastoral wisdom rather than a rigid rule-list. Claude grounds this in the observed divergence between Matthew's exception and Mark/Luke's absolute wording plus the Mosaic-concession precedent; Local's parallel 'situational/pastoral discretion' entry argued from theological inference and silence but supplied no direct citation of its own.
positions.why_not_higher: This view infers pastoral flexibility from textual variation between Gospels and from silence, rather than from an explicit statement granting such discretion, making it more speculative than the exception-based positions; the Local voice's contribution to this cluster cited no supporting evidence at all, which weakens the combined case.
positions.confidence_rationale: Plausible as a redaction-critical and pastoral-theological observation, but it argues partly from silence; one contributing voice's version of this position had an empty evidence array, a sparse-grounding limitation that should not be smoothed over.
positions.weakest_link: Lacks a direct statement (such as a Pauline pastoral-application passage) in the retrieved evidence that would confirm intentional discretion rather than simple textual variance; the Local voice could offer no verse at all for its version of this claim.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit pastoral-application texts (e.g., material addressing desertion or mixed marriages, such as 1 Corinthians 7) would substantially strengthen or weaken this reading depending on their content.
interpretive_moves: Treats Matthew's added exception clause as evidence the early church contextualized Jesus's teaching for its community's needs
Draws an analogy from Moses's own legal concession as precedent for scripture accommodating hardness of heart
Reads NT silence on an exhaustive exception-list as implicit permission for contextual, conscience-guided application

nodes.label: Ideal held with contextual application
nodes.detail: Lifelong marriage is the norm, but application varies by context and pastoral wisdom.

nodes.label: Mosaic concession precedent
nodes.detail: Moses' certificate of divorce shows scripture accommodating human hardness in specific settings.

nodes.label: Absolute sayings resist relativizing
nodes.detail: Mark and Luke present Jesus's words as flat prohibition, not merely situational guidance.

evidence.quote: They asked him, “Why then did Moses command us to give her a certificate of divorce and divorce her?”
evidence.reasoning: The Pharisees' question shows divorce practice was already a matter of legal/pastoral debate before Jesus's ruling.

evidence.quote: They said, “Moses allowed a certificate of divorce to be written, and to divorce her.”
evidence.reasoning: Establishes that scripture itself contains a historical precedent of accommodating divorce under certain conditions; borrowed here to give the merged position (including Local's uncited sub-claim) a grounded citation per synthesis rule 13.

source_position_labels: Normative ideal with pastoral/situational discretion in application (Claude Code sonnet)
Situational/pastoral discretion view (Local granite4.1:8b — originally cited zero supporting evidence; evidence borrowed from the Claude Code cluster per synthesis rule 13)

positions.label: Broad 'hardness of heart' concession model (expanded divorce grounds)
positions.summary: NT divorce sayings are read as pastoral concessions addressing specific abuses, implying a range of serious marital breakdowns — not just adultery or desertion — can justify divorce and remarriage. Named only by the Local voice; preserved as dissent.
positions.why_not_higher: Interpretation relies on contextual reading of Mark 10:2-12 rather than a single definitive NT verse endorsing broad grounds, and was not independently corroborated by the second voice.
positions.confidence_rationale: Medium-to-low confidence due to reliance on broader contextual interpretation rather than direct scriptural enumeration, and single-voice attestation.
positions.weakest_link: No singular NT passage explicitly lists multiple valid grounds beyond adultery and desertion.
positions.what_would_change_this: Discovery of a NT text enumerating additional legitimate reasons for divorce would bolster this view.
interpretive_moves: Reading Mark 10:2-12 as addressing societal hardness rather than prescribing exhaustive rules
Inferring pastoral flexibility from Jesus' response to the Pharisees

nodes.label: Divorce permitted for broader marital abuses

evidence.quote: Pharisees came to him testing him, and asked him, “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife?”
evidence.reasoning: Initiates discussion on divorce law.

evidence.quote: They said, “Moses allowed a certificate of divorce to be written, and to divorce her.”
evidence.reasoning: Acknowledges Moses' concession as a response to hardness of heart.

evidence.quote: If a woman herself divorces her husband and marries another, she commits adultery.”
evidence.reasoning: Cited by the originating voice as still constraining remarriage even within a broader-grounds reading.

source_position_labels: Broad 'hardness of heart' concession model (Local granite4.1:8b only — Claude Code referenced the same Mosaic-concession text but only as a supporting move within its discretion position, not as a standalone expanded-grounds claim)

synthesis.dissent_notes: Two positions survive on single-voice attestation per rule 3: (1) 'Exception justifies divorce only, not remarriage' was named only by Claude Code and is preserved at half its original weight (0.2 → 0.1); (2) 'Broad hardness-of-heart concession model' (expanded divorce grounds beyond adultery) was named only by Local and is preserved at half its original weight (0.1765 → 0.088). The margin between the top position ('Exception permits both divorce and remarriage,' 0.337) and the third-largest ('Pastoral/situational discretion,' 0.281) is narrow (~0.056), and the discretion cluster is partly propped up by a Local sub-position that cited zero supporting evidence — flagged rather than smoothed over. Both voices independently noted that classic proof-texts central to this debate in the wider tradition — 1 Corinthians 7:15 (the Pauline/desertion 'not under bondage' exception), Matthew 19:8 ('hardness of your hearts'), and Romans 7:2-3 — are absent from the retrieved evidence entirely; as a result, the historically significant Pauline-privilege position could not be scored at all in this distribution and is not represented among the final positions. Local's raw weights (summing to 1.7 before its own internal renormalization) suggest that voice treated its four positions as somewhat overlapping framings rather than a strict mutually-exclusive partition, which is worth noting for audit purposes even though its final normalized weights were used for averaging.
unresolved_tensions: Matthew 19:9 contains an explicit sexual-immorality exception clause while the parallel sayings in Mark 10:11-12 and Luke 16:18 state the prohibition without qualification.
It is disputed whether the Matthean exception clause licenses remarriage or only excuses the divorce itself.
Whether 'sexual immorality' (porneia) in Matt 19:9 means general marital infidelity or a narrower category (e.g., incestuous unions) is not resolved by the retrieved verses.
Whether abandonment or abuse constitutes a valid ground for NT-permitted divorce beyond sexual immorality is unresolved and rests on inference rather than direct text.
The extent to which pastoral judgment can override or supplement explicit NT exceptions is unresolved.

Key texts often central to this debate (1 Corinthians 7, Matthew 19:8, Romans 7:2-3) are absent from the retrieved evidence, leaving major traditional arguments — especially the Pauline desertion exception — unaddressed by either voice.
synthesis.synthesis: Both voices agree the interpretive fork runs through the same textual seam: Matthew 19:9's explicit sexual-immorality exception versus Mark 10:11-12 and Luke 16:18's unqualified 'commits adultery' language. Where they converge most strongly is in treating the exception-permits-remarriage reading and the strict-indissolubility reading as the two best-attested positions, and in independently arriving at a third, softer position — that the Synoptic divergence itself signals pastoral/situational discretion in application rather than a single mechanical rule. They diverge in emphasis and confidence: Claude Code treats the divorce-only-not-remarriage reading as a serious minority grammatical position worth naming, while Local instead surfaces a distinct 'broad hardness-of-heart' claim that the concession language implies wider grounds than adultery alone, and its discretion-view sub-position rests on inference from silence with no cited verse. The deeper tension both voices flag but cannot resolve from the retrieved evidence is that the debate's classic tie-breaking texts — 1 Corinthians 7:15's Pauline privilege, Matthew 19:8's 'hardness of heart' rationale, and Romans 7:2-3 — never surfaced in retrieval, so no voice could adjudicate the scope question (does Matthew's exception govern remarriage, or only the divorce?) on textual grounds beyond the Synoptic sayings themselves. The retrieval set also contained substantial off-topic 'new testament'-as-covenant noise (Hebrews, Last Supper sayings) that both voices correctly filtered out.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: Both contributing voices independently reported 'medium' confidence, which sets the ceiling here. Direct, repeated Synoptic sayings on divorce (Matt 19:9, Mark 10:2-12, Luke 16:18) are present and support multiple defensible positions, but key cross-referenced texts (1 Cor 7, Matt 19:8, Rom 7) that traditions typically use to adjudicate between these readings are missing from the retrieved evidence entirely. The retrieval set also contained substantial off-topic noise (Hebrews/Last-Supper 'testament' verses, correctly ignored by both voices). Additionally, one voice's 'situational/pastoral discretion' position was submitted with an empty evidence array — a visible sparse-grounding gap folded into the merged discretion cluster rather than hidden — and the top two-to-three positions sit within a relatively narrow weight band (0.337 / 0.281 / 0.194), so confidence is kept at medium rather than high.

Central unqualified divorce-remarriage-adultery saying; supports the indissolubility position and is cited as challenging evidence against the exception and discretion positions.
Parallel wife-to-husband saying; supports the indissolubility, divorce-only, and broad hardness-of-heart positions.
Independent Gospel tradition confirming the unqualified prohibition; supports the indissolubility position and challenges the exception and discretion positions.
Refers to the 'new testament' as covenant in Hebrews' argument about Christ's priesthood; a lexical retrieval false-positive, unrelated to marriage law.
Discusses the first covenant's dedication with blood, unrelated to marriage law.
Refers to the blood of the covenant at Sinai, unrelated to divorce and remarriage.
Discusses Christ as mediator of the new covenant/testament, unrelated to marriage.
Discusses the legal principle of a testament requiring the testator's death, unrelated to marriage law.
Discusses the legal force of a testament after death, unrelated to marriage law.

positions.label: Exception for sexual immorality permits both divorce and remarriage
positions.summary: Matthew's account of Jesus's teaching explicitly carves out sexual immorality (porneia) as a legitimate ground for divorce, and the clause's placement is read as extending to remarriage rights as well.
positions.why_not_higher: Mark and Luke record the same core teaching of Jesus without any exception clause at all, so this view must explain why Matthew alone preserves a qualifier the other Synoptics omit.
positions.confidence_rationale: Direct verbal support exists (Matt 19:9) but the parallel Gospel silence and grammatical ambiguity keep this from being decisive on its own.
positions.weakest_link: Whether 'except for sexual immorality' grammatically licenses the following 'and marries another' clause, or only excuses the divorce itself, is disputed among careful readers of the Greek.
positions.what_would_change_this: A clearer parallel text (e.g., explicit remarriage language tied to the exception, or 1 Corinthians 7 material) would settle the scope question.
interpretive_moves: Matthew is read as clarifying/completing Mark's more absolute-sounding saying rather than conflicting with it
Porneia is read broadly as marital sexual unfaithfulness (adultery) rather than narrowly as incest
The exception clause is taken to modify the whole sentence, including remarriage, not just the act of divorcing

result.dissent_notes: Two well-attested historic positions cannot be scored as standalone entries because their key proof-texts are absent from the retrieved evidence: (1) the Pauline/desertion exception, normally grounded in 1 Corinthians 7:15 ('not under bondage' when an unbeliever departs), has no corresponding verse in this evidence set; (2) the broad 'hardness of heart' concession model relies on Matthew 19:8's phrase 'because of the hardness of your hearts,' which is likewise not present here. Both are real, seriously held positions in the wider Christian tradition and should be treated as under-evidenced here rather than refuted.
unresolved_tensions: Matthew 19:9 contains an explicit sexual-immorality exception clause while the parallel sayings in Mark 10:11-12 and Luke 16:18 state the prohibition without qualification.
It is disputed whether the Matthean exception clause licenses remarriage or only excuses the divorce itself.
Whether 'sexual immorality' (porneia) in Matt 19:9 means general marital infidelity or a narrower category (e.g., incestuous unions) is not resolved by the retrieved verses.
Key texts often central to this debate (1 Corinthians 7, Matthew 19:8, Romans 7:2-3) are absent from the retrieved evidence, leaving major traditional arguments unaddressed.
result.synthesis: The strongest indissolubility case rests on Mark 10:11-12 and Luke 16:18's flatly unqualified 'commits adultery' language, read as universal; the strongest exception-with-remarriage case rests on Matthew 19:9's explicit porneia clause read as covering the whole sentence including remarriage; a middle position agrees an exception justifies divorce but denies it extends to remarriage, hinging on a narrower grammatical reading of the same clause; the pastoral-discretion view treats the Synoptic divergence itself as evidence of contextual application. The central tension is whether Matthew's exception clause is read as normative and remarriage-permitting, or as a narrower/secondary qualifier subordinate to Mark and Luke's absolute wording — a question the retrieved evidence raises sharply but does not itself resolve.
result.confidence_rationale: Direct, repeated Synoptic sayings on divorce (Matt 19:9, Mark 10:2-12, Luke 16:18) are present and support multiple defensible positions, but key cross-referenced texts (1 Cor 7, Matt 19:8, Rom 7) that traditions typically use to adjudicate between these readings are missing from the retrieved evidence, and the retrieval set also contains substantial off-topic noise (Hebrews 'testament' verses).

positions.label: No divorce, no remarriage
positions.summary: Marriage is indissoluble for any reason; civil divorce does not affect biblical marital status, and remarriage while a spouse lives is never permitted.
positions.why_not_higher: Only two verses directly support this strict view; many NT passages allow divorce under specific conditions.
positions.confidence_rationale: Limited to direct citations; other NT texts provide exceptions.
positions.weakest_link: Lacks broader scriptural support beyond two verses.

result.synthesis: The New Testament presents a normative ideal of lifelong marriage, with explicit allowance for divorce only in cases of sexual immorality (Matt 19:9). Mark 10:11 and Luke 16:18 reinforce the prohibition of remarriage after divorce. Interpretive traditions diverge on whether additional grounds (e.g., abandonment, abuse) are biblically sanctioned, leading to a range of views from strict indissolubility to broad pastoral discretion.
result.confidence_rationale: Multiple NT passages directly address divorce, but the lack of an exhaustive list of permissible grounds leaves room for interpretive variation, resulting in medium confidence.

kill_test.target_label: Exception for sexual immorality permits both divorce and remarriage
kill_test.strongest_counter: Luke 16:18 states unequivocally that anyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, without any exception for sexual immorality. This verse directly contradicts the leading position's claim that an exception for sexual immorality permits remarriage. The absolute prohibition in Luke leaves no room for a qualifying clause like 'except for sexual immorality,' making the exception argument untenable.
kill_test.vulnerable_claim: The grammatical interpretation that 'except for sexual immorality' licenses remarriage (as found in Matthew 19:9) is vulnerable to being dismissed by the absolute statement in Luke 16:18, which does not contain such an exception.
kill_test.notes: The fatal contradiction between Luke 16:18 and the exception clause in Matthew 19:9 nullifies the leading position's core claim.

positions.label: Insufficient Retrieved Evidence to Adjudicate the Israel–Church Question
positions.summary: Neither voice's retrieval set surfaced the classic proof texts for this debate (Romans 9-11, Galatians 3:29/6:16, Ephesians 2:11-22, 1 Peter 2:9-10, Matthew 21:43); the available verses are either historical-narrative OT war/treaty material about national Israel or NT verses that use 'church' only administratively, with no verse directly relating the two entities theologically.
positions.why_not_higher: Held at just over half the total weight because it is a procedural placeholder about evidentiary sufficiency, not a positive theological claim; a second voice (Local/granite) nonetheless found enough traction in the same evidence pool to score all six traditional positions, so this cannot claim the full distribution.
positions.confidence_rationale: The retrieval set is dominated by unrelated OT military/political texts and NT verses that only incidentally use 'church'; the standard anchor texts for the Israel-church debate are entirely absent from both voices' evidence.
positions.weakest_link: The position is built on absence of evidence rather than a positive argument, so on its own it cannot adjudicate between the six named traditions.
positions.what_would_change_this: Retrieving Romans 11:17-26 (olive tree), Galatians 6:16 ('Israel of God'), Ephesians 2:11-22 ('one new man'), 1 Peter 2:9-10, and Matthew 21:43 would let each candidate position be scored on its actual anchor texts.
interpretive_moves: Distinguish 'church' used merely as a lexical/institutional term (1 Cor 16:19, Acts 15:41) from 'church' used in a theological argument about identity relative to Israel.
Recognize OT verses about Israel's wars with neighboring nations as historical-narrative, carrying no ecclesiological freight.
Require a verse to make an explicit theological claim, not just incidental word overlap, before citing it for a doctrinal position.

positions.label: Dispensational Distinction
positions.summary: Israel and the church are distinct peoples with separate identities, promises, and destinies that remain permanently unconflated.
positions.why_not_higher: The evidence for a clear biblical distinction is limited to historical conflict narratives and lacks explicit New Testament affirmation of perpetual separation; only one voice named this position, halving its cluster weight, and it was further boosted only by renormalization after two zero-evidence positions were removed.
positions.confidence_rationale: Medium-low per the contributing voice — relies on Old Testament war accounts and typological inference; New Testament verses in the retrieved set are ambiguous regarding permanent separation.
positions.weakest_link: Absence of direct New Testament teaching in the retrieved evidence affirming an eternal, unchanging split between Israel and the church.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit New Testament statements (e.g. from Romans 9-11) affirming a perpetual dual-people structure would strengthen this view.
interpretive_moves: Read Old Testament national narratives as typological precursors of future distinct peoples.
Interpret New Testament references to 'Israel' as primarily ethnic, not universal.

positions.label: Covenant Theology / Supersessionism (Replacement)
positions.summary: The church is the new or true Israel, inheriting and fulfilling promises originally given to national Israel, which no longer holds a distinct theological status.
positions.why_not_higher: New Testament passages in the retrieved set suggest continuity but were not written to make an Israel-versus-church argument; Old Testament references to Israel's history complicate a total-replacement reading. Named by only one voice.
positions.confidence_rationale: Medium-low per the contributing voice — some NT support for church-as-fulfillment, but the underlying texts don't directly address supersession.
positions.weakest_link: Old Testament verses like Zechariah 11:14 imply a broken relationship rather than clear complete supersession.
positions.what_would_change_this: Clear New Testament declarations (e.g. from Galatians 6:16 or Romans 9-11) that national Israel has been fully absorbed into the church would elevate this view.
interpretive_moves: Interpret 'church' as the universal people of God, encompassing all ethnic groups.
Read Old Testament prophecies as fulfilled in Christ's body.

evidence.reasoning: Emphasizes the church as a distinct entity worthy of presentation.

positions.label: Fulfillment Theology (Typological Continuity without Replacement)
positions.summary: The church fulfills and extends Israel's identity and promises in Christ, without implying ethnic Israel is rejected or erased as a category.
positions.why_not_higher: Relies heavily on interpretive typology with limited direct biblical affirmation of continuity-without-replacement in the retrieved set; named by only one voice.
positions.confidence_rationale: Low per the contributing voice — the typological reading is plausible but under-evidenced here.
positions.weakest_link: New Testament passages explicitly addressing 'church' in the retrieved set suggest separation rather than pure continuity.
positions.what_would_change_this: Explicit New Testament texts (e.g. Romans 11) affirming the church as continuation of Israel's covenant promises would bolster this view.
interpretive_moves: Read Old Testament prophecies as fulfilled in Christ and his body.
Interpret 'Israel' in New Testament contexts as primarily ethnic, but see its spiritual fulfillment in believers.

positions.label: Two-Covenant / Dual-Track Salvation View
positions.summary: Israel remains in a valid, separate covenant relationship with God apart from Christ, while the church constitutes a distinct path for Gentiles (and Jewish believers).
positions.why_not_higher: Only one retrieved verse hints at ongoing distinct ministry; named by only one voice and supported by a single, non-decisive citation.
positions.confidence_rationale: Low per the contributing voice — sparse direct biblical support for a dual-covenant salvation model in this evidence set.
positions.weakest_link: Lack of explicit New Testament affirmation in the retrieved evidence that Gentiles and ethnic Jews follow separate salvific tracks.
positions.what_would_change_this: Clear New Testament passages affirming distinct salvific pathways for Israel and the church would strengthen this view.
interpretive_moves: Separate 'church' from 'Israel' in covenantal terms.
Interpret Acts 15:41 as evidence of ongoing Jewish outreach distinct from Gentile church formation.

nodes.label: Israel and the church have separate salvific covenants.
Church as a universal body distinct from Israel's covenant.

evidence.quote: And he went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the churches.
evidence.reasoning: Suggests ongoing ministry that this voice reads as distinct from Israel's covenant context.

synthesis.dissent_notes: Two positions from Local (granite4.1:8b) — 'Olive Tree / Israel-and-Church-as-One-People Model' and 'Progressive Covenantalism' — were dropped from the scored final positions because that voice supplied them with empty evidence and empty supporting_evidence_ids arrays (a rule-13 violation if carried forward uncited); they are preserved instead in unresolved_tensions below. Their combined weight (~9.1% of Local's distribution) was redistributed proportionally across the remaining positions during renormalization, which is why weights here are slightly higher than a naive halving of each voice's original numbers would produce. More fundamentally, the two voices disagree in kind, not just in weight: Claude Code (sonnet) declined to score any of the six traditional positions at all, arguing the retrieval set lacks the debate's actual proof texts (Romans 9-11, Galatians 3:29/6:16, Ephesians 2:11-22, 1 Peter 2:9-10, Matthew 21:43) and that citing them would require invented evidence; Local (granite4.1:8b) scored all six positions using that same sparse, largely tangential evidence pool despite also rating its own confidence low. This synthesis honors both stances by giving 'Insufficient Evidence' the largest single share (0.55) while still preserving Local's scored positions at reduced, dissent-adjusted weights. Margins among the four retained named traditions are narrow (0.15 / 0.125 / 0.10 / 0.075), so none should be read as a clear consensus front-runner among classic doctrinal camps.
unresolved_tensions: Whether Romans 11's olive-tree metaphor (absent from both voices' retrieved evidence) implies one continuous people of God or a temporary Gentile branch grafted alongside a still-distinct Israel — flagged by one voice as a position (Olive Tree model) but removed here for lack of any cited verse.
Whether 'Israel of God' in Galatians 6:16 refers to the church or to ethnic Jewish believers — a foundational exegetical fork neither voice's evidence set touches.
Whether Ephesians 2's 'one new man' language entails full identity-merger or merely reconciled coexistence — unresolved without that passage present in either voice's evidence.

Progressive Covenantalism (mediating fulfillment-in-Christ view, agnostic about ethnic Israel's future) was proposed by one voice with zero citations and could not be retained as a scored position; whether it should be reintroduced once proper proof texts are retrieved remains open.

The extent of Israel's future national restoration versus its replacement by the church, and the interpretive weight of Old Testament prophetic language (e.g. Zechariah 11:14) about a 'broken brotherhood,' remain contested across the scored positions.
synthesis.synthesis: Both voices agree that the retrieval set actually gathered is thin and largely off-target for this classic debate: it consists mostly of Old Testament war/treaty narratives about national Israel and New Testament verses that mention 'church' only administratively, with none of the field's standard anchor texts (Romans 9-11, Galatians 3:29/6:16, Ephesians 2:11-22, 1 Peter 2:9-10, Matthew 21:43) present. Where the voices diverge is methodological: Claude Code (sonnet) treats this absence as disqualifying and refuses to score any of the six traditional positions, collapsing everything into a single 'insufficient evidence' finding; Local (granite4.1:8b) instead extends interpretive inference to the same weak evidence pool and produces a full ranked distribution across Dispensationalism, Supersessionism, Fulfillment Theology, the Two-Covenant view, the Olive Tree model, and Progressive Covenantalism — while itself rating confidence low throughout. The synthesis preserves both postures: it grants the largest weight to the 'insufficient evidence' verdict as the more evidentially conservative and jointly-attested caution, while still surfacing Local's four best-evidenced named traditions (Dispensational, Supersessionist, Fulfillment, Two-Covenant) at reduced weights, since dissenting positions from a single voice must survive per the preservation rule. The two positions Local supplied with zero citations were not retained as scored claims, only as open questions, since an uncited doctrinal position cannot be responsibly weighted alongside cited ones.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: Both contributing voices independently rated their own confidence 'low,' and for the same underlying reason: the retrieval set never surfaced the debate's classic proof texts (Romans 9-11, Galatians 3/6, Ephesians 2, 1 Peter 2, Matthew 21). One voice treated this as disqualifying for all six traditional positions; the other scored them anyway but flagged sparse, largely tangential support (OT war narratives, incidental NT 'church' references) for every position, including two positions with no citations at all. Per rule 9, the lowest confidence among voices contributing major positions governs, and per rule 12 this gap is surfaced directly rather than smoothed over.

nodes.label: Argument from silence
nodes.detail: Concluding 'insufficient evidence' is itself not a substantive theological claim.

evidence.quote: The churches of Asia salute you... with the church that is in their house.
evidence.reasoning: Uses 'church' as a social/institutional term with no theological statement about Israel.

evidence.quote: He went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the churches.
evidence.reasoning: References local congregations administratively, offering no doctrine of Israel-church continuity or discontinuity.

evidence.quote: That he might present the church to himself a glorious church... holy and without blemish.
evidence.reasoning: Describes the church's sanctified identity but does not compare or relate it to Israel's covenants or promises.

result.dissent_notes: The six scoped positions (Dispensational Distinction, Covenant Theology/Supersessionism, Fulfillment Theology, Two-Covenant view, Olive Tree model, Progressive Covenantalism) are all real, defensible traditions, but none can be cited from this evidence set: their standard textual anchors — Romans 9-11 (esp. 11:17-26 olive tree, 11:26 'all Israel will be saved'), Galatians 3:29 and 6:16 ('Israel of God'), Ephesians 2:11-22 ('one new man'), 1 Peter 2:9-10, and Matthew 21:43 — were not retrieved. Presenting weighted positions on these texts would require inventing citations, which is prohibited.
unresolved_tensions: Whether Romans 11's olive-tree metaphor implies one continuous people of God or a temporary Gentile branch grafted alongside a still-distinct Israel remains the central unresolved question, and it cannot be examined without Romans 11 in evidence.
Whether 'Israel of God' in Galatians 6:16 refers to the church or to ethnic Jewish believers is a foundational exegetical fork this evidence set does not touch.
Whether Ephesians 2's 'one new man' language entails full identity-merger or merely reconciled coexistence is unresolved without that passage present.
result.synthesis: The retrieved evidence set consists almost entirely of unrelated Old Testament war/treaty narratives involving national Israel and New Testament verses that mention 'church' only in passing administrative contexts; none directly engages the Israel-church relationship, so no weighted comparison among dispensationalism, supersessionism, fulfillment theology, the two-covenant view, the olive-tree model, or progressive covenantalism can be responsibly grounded in what was provided.
result.confidence_rationale: Confidence is low because the retrieval failed to surface any of the passages central to this debate (Romans 9-11, Galatians 3/6, Ephesians 2, 1 Peter 2, Matthew 21); citing the six candidate positions against the actual evidence here would require unsupported inference beyond what the text states.
